entry.title: Winters and Springs: AI from Dartmouth to Deep Learning
entry.summary: The field's booms and busts were never really about the science — they tracked the money, and so does the one we are living in now.
entry.content:
A History of Artificial Intelligence · Part 1

Winters and Springs: AI from Dartmouth to Deep Learning

Six decades of AI ran hot and cold on a single rhythm — promises drew money, money outran results, and the funding fled.

11 min read2026-07-07

A field that freezes and thaws

The history of artificial intelligence is usually told as a story of ideas — perceptrons, expert systems, neural networks — arriving and being proven wrong or right. That telling misses the engine underneath. AI has always advanced when three things collided: a good idea, hardware fast enough to run it, and money willing to wait. When any one failed, the field froze. Practitioners even have a name for these freezes, borrowed from the nuclear-winter metaphor of the 1980s: AI winters.

AI winter A period of collapsed funding and interest following a wave of over-promising. There have been two full ones — roughly 1974–1980 and 1987–1993 — each triggered less by a failure of science than by a failure of results to justify the money already spent.

This first part of our history runs from the summer the field was named, in 1956, to the winter afternoon in 2012 when it stopped freezing. The spine below is a timeline; read the dates as the plot. The argument threaded through it is simple and, by the end, should feel inevitable: the winters were caused by money, and understanding that is the only way to read the enormous sums being spent on AI today.1

The spine: 1956–2012

Fifteen turning points, the major ones marked. Watch how each spring is funded by someone betting on a promise, and each winter arrives when the promise comes due.

  1. 1956

    The Dartmouth workshop names the field1

    John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Nathaniel Rochester and Claude Shannon convene a summer study at Dartmouth, proposing that "every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can in principle be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it." Their 1955 proposal coins the term "artificial intelligence" and launches the field as an organized program.

  2. 1958

    Rosenblatt's Perceptron, and the first hype2

    Frank Rosenblatt, funded by the US Office of Naval Research, builds the Mark I Perceptron — the first trainable neural network. The press, and Rosenblatt himself, oversell it wildly; a machine that could tell left from right becomes, in the newspapers, the dawn of thinking machines. The gap between demo and headline is a pattern the field never outgrows.

  3. 1966

    The ALPAC report kills machine translation3

    A US National Research Council committee concludes that machine translation is slower, less accurate and more expensive than a human translator. Government funding for the field is gutted almost overnight — the first time a sober audit of results collapses a subfield's money. It would not be the last.

  4. 1969

    Minsky and Papert freeze the perceptron4

    In Perceptrons, Minsky and Papert prove that a single-layer network cannot compute even the simple XOR function. The result is mathematically narrow but rhetorically devastating; neural-network funding withers for over a decade as the field pivots to symbolic, rule-based AI.

  5. 1973

    The Lighthill Report triggers the first winter5

    Commissioned by the UK Science Research Council, mathematician James Lighthill argues that AI has failed to scale beyond "toy" problems because of the combinatorial explosion — the search space growing exponentially faster than any computer could keep up. British AI funding is cut to all but a couple of universities. McCarthy publishes a rebuttal, but the money is already gone.6

  6. 1974–80

    DARPA pulls back; the US freezes too7

    The Mansfield Amendment forces defense research toward direct military relevance, and DARPA, disappointed by a costly speech-understanding program at Carnegie Mellon, stops writing blank checks for open-ended AI. The founding generation's easy money is over on both sides of the Atlantic.

  7. Notice the shape of the first winter. No theorem was disproved in 1974; the ideas of 1956 were still there. What changed was that the funders — DARPA, the UK research councils — looked at a decade of spending and could not find the promised payoff. The thaw, when it came, would be led not by a better idea but by a business case.

  8. 1980

    Expert systems make AI pay7

    Digital Equipment Corporation deploys XCON (originally R1), a rule-based "expert system" that configures VAX computer orders and is reported to save the company tens of millions of dollars a year. Suddenly AI has revenue, and a whole industry of expert-system software and specialized LISP-machine hardware springs up around it.

  9. 1982

    Japan bets the house: the Fifth Generation8

    Japan's Ministry of International Trade and Industry launches the Fifth Generation Computer Systems project, spending a little under ¥57 billion (roughly US$320 million) over a decade on logic-programming supercomputers. It spooks Washington and London into competitive responses — a funding race, run on national anxiety.

  10. 1983

    America answers with a billion dollars9

    DARPA launches the Strategic Computing Initiative, pouring roughly US$1 billion over ten years into AI, chip design and autonomous systems. The 1980s boom is now a government-and-venture-funded arms race, its ambitions running well ahead of what the hardware of the day could deliver.10

  11. 1986

    Backpropagation quietly plants the future12

    David Rumelhart, Geoffrey Hinton and Ronald Williams popularize backpropagation in Nature, giving multi-layer neural networks a practical way to learn — the exact capability the 1969 critique said they lacked. Almost no one notices; the money is all in symbolic AI and LISP machines. The seed of the next spring is planted at the height of the boom that is about to collapse.

  12. 1987

    The LISP-machine market collapses11

    Cheap general-purpose workstations from Sun and PCs from the mass market overtake specialized LISP hardware. Symbolics, whose revenue had peaked around US$114 million in 1986, slides toward bankruptcy; Lisp Machines Inc. fails outright. Expert systems prove brittle and expensive to maintain, DARPA cuts AI funding sharply, and the second winter sets in.7

  13. The second winter (roughly 1987–1993) rhymes with the first: a hardware business, a wave of corporate optimism, and government money all failed at once, and none of it was because the underlying research was wrong. Through the freeze, a small band kept working on neural networks — and on the unglamorous benchmarks and datasets that would later matter more than anyone guessed.

  14. 1997

    Deep Blue beats Kasparov14

    IBM's Deep Blue defeats world chess champion Garry Kasparov 3.5–2.5, searching some 200 million positions per second. It is a landmark — but a landmark of brute-force symbolic search, not learning. The methods that would define the modern era were still in the cold.

  15. 1998

    LeCun's convolutional nets learn to see13

    Yann LeCun and colleagues publish LeNet-5, a convolutional neural network that reads handwritten digits end-to-end, alongside the MNIST benchmark. It works, it is trainable, and — for want of data and compute — it stays a niche result for another fourteen years.

  16. 2009

    ImageNet builds the fuel15

    Fei-Fei Li and collaborators release ImageNet, a labeled database of millions of images organized by concept, and later a public competition to classify them. The idea that data, not just algorithms, is the scarce ingredient is about to be proven at a stroke.

  17. 2012

    AlexNet ends the winter for good16

    Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever and Geoffrey Hinton train a deep convolutional network on two consumer gaming GPUs and win the ImageNet competition with a top-5 error of 15.3%, against 26.2% for the runner-up — a margin so large it ended the argument. The idea (1986), the data (2009) and the hardware (GPUs) had finally arrived together. The spring that followed has not yet ended.

The winters were made of money

Line the busts up and the common cause is not intellectual but financial. The ALPAC report of 1966 did not prove translation impossible; it proved it was not yet worth the price, and the funding vanished.3 Lighthill did not refute AI; he judged that a decade of spending had not scaled, and the UK cut the cheques.5 The 1980s boom was, at its core, a capital-allocation event: Japan committed roughly ¥57 billion to the Fifth Generation project,8 and DARPA answered with about a billion US dollars through the Strategic Computing Initiative.9

That boom died the way overbuilt hardware markets always die. Specialized LISP machines were premium-priced bets on a future that cheaper, general-purpose computers reached first; when Symbolics — a company that had booked around US$114 million in revenue at its 1986 peak — could no longer justify its machines against a Sun workstation, the business evaporated, and the expert-system industry built on top of it went with it.11 DARPA, having seen its Strategic Computing ambitions outrun the silicon, pulled back hard.7 No new theorem was needed to cause the second winter; a spreadsheet did the job.

The ideas were rarely the problem. The bill coming due, before the results could cover it, was.

The through-line matters because it reframes what "progress" in AI has meant. The seed of the modern era — backpropagation for deep networks — was fully described in 1986, at the height of the boom that was about to bust, and then sat unused for a generation.12 What changed by 2012 was not the idea but its inputs: enough labeled data15 and cheap parallel hardware to make the idea finally pay. AI does not advance when someone thinks of something; it advances when the idea, the data and the money line up at once.

Echoes today

Here is the specific parallel to carry out of this history. The AI boom of the mid-2020s is, like every prior spring, a money story before it is a science story — hundreds of billions of dollars a year in data-center and chip spending, underwritten by the belief that the returns will arrive. That belief is exactly what failed in 1974 and 1987. The open question is whether this time the results cover the bill.

The clearest echo is in the hardware. The 1980s bust was, mechanically, a collapse in the value of specialized AI machines that the market had bought on optimistic assumptions. In 2025, the investor Michael Burry made precisely that analogy, calling Nvidia "a Cisco at the center" of the AI buildout and arguing that hyperscalers are depreciating their GPUs over four-to-six years when the true useful life, given Nvidia's annual product cadence, is closer to two-and-a-half to three-and-a-half — understating costs and setting up writedowns.17 Whether or not he is right, the shape of the worry is old: specialized compute, bought on hopeful accounting, is the same bet that sank the LISP-machine makers.11

The reader who has absorbed this history should watch one variable above all: not the capability of the models, which improves steadily, but the ratio of money spent to money returned. Every winter began when that ratio broke. Nothing in the record says a third winter is inevitable — but nothing says the pattern has been repealed, either.

Go deeper

Start at the source: the 1955 Dartmouth proposal is two pages and reads like a founding charter.1 For the first winter, read Lighthill's survey against McCarthy's rebuttal as a pair — a rare case of a field's fate argued in public.56 Roland and Shiman's history of the Strategic Computing Initiative is the definitive account of how a billion dollars of AI ambition met the limits of 1980s hardware.10 And to feel the hinge of 2012 for yourself, the AlexNet paper is short and its results speak plainly.16 Next in this series: The Scaling Era, which picks up where AlexNet leaves off and asks what happens when, for the first time, more money reliably buys more intelligence.

Sources

  1. AI Magazine (AAAI) — McCarthy, Minsky, Rochester & Shannon, "A Proposal for the Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence" (1955, reprinted 2006). The founding proposal; the coining of "artificial intelligence."
  2. Psychological Review (via PubMed) — Rosenblatt, "The perceptron: a probabilistic model for information storage and organization in the brain" (1958). The first trainable neural network.
  3. National Academies Press — ALPAC, "Language and Machines: Computers in Translation and Linguistics" (1966). The report that ended machine-translation funding.
  4. Wikipedia — "Perceptrons (book)," Minsky & Papert (1969). The XOR limitation and its chilling effect on neural-network funding.
  5. Wikipedia — "Lighthill report" (1973). The combinatorial-explosion critique, the funding cuts, and the BBC debate that triggered the first winter.
  6. Stanford (John McCarthy) — "Review of 'Artificial Intelligence: A General Survey'". McCarthy's contemporaneous rebuttal to Lighthill.
  7. Wikipedia — "AI winter". The Mansfield Amendment and DARPA pullback, XCON's savings, the 1987 LISP-machine collapse, and DARPA's second-winter cuts.
  8. Wikipedia — "Fifth Generation Computer Systems". Japan's 1982 project and its ~¥57 billion (~US$320M) spend.
  9. Wikipedia — "Strategic Computing Initiative". DARPA's 1983–1993 program and its roughly US$1 billion budget.
  10. MIT Press — Roland & Shiman, "Strategic Computing: DARPA and the Quest for Machine Intelligence, 1983–1993". The authoritative account of the SCI.
  11. MIT OpenCourseWare — Graylin et al., "Symbolics, Inc.: A Failure of Heterogeneous Engineering". Symbolics' rise, ~US$114M peak revenue, and the LISP-machine market's collapse.
  12. Nature — Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams, "Learning representations by back-propagating errors" (1986). Backpropagation for multi-layer networks — the seed of deep learning.
  13. Proceedings of the IEEE — LeCun, Bottou, Bengio & Haffner, "Gradient-Based Learning Applied to Document Recognition" (LeNet-5, 1998). The convolutional network and the MNIST benchmark.
  14. IBM — "Deep Blue". The May 1997 defeat of Garry Kasparov, 3.5–2.5.
  15. CVPR 2009 / ImageNet — Deng, Dong, Socher, Li, Li & Fei-Fei, "ImageNet: A Large-Scale Hierarchical Image Database". The dataset that made the 2012 breakthrough measurable.
  16. NeurIPS 2012 — Krizhevsky, Sutskever & Hinton, "ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks" (AlexNet). The 15.3%-vs-26.2% top-5 result on GPUs that ended the winter.
  17. Fortune — "'Big Short' investor Michael Burry on an AI bubble: 'There is a Cisco at the center of it all… Its name is Nvidia'" (2025). The GPU-depreciation and hardware-overhang argument echoing the LISP-machine bust.

entry.title: Nvidia: The Toll Booth of the AI Era
entry.summary: A $194-billion data-center business defended by a software moat two decades deep — and a single government the moat cannot patch.
entry.content:
Frontier Compute

Nvidia: The Toll Booth of the AI Era

Nvidia sells the shovels for the AI gold rush — the question for an owner is how long the miners stay locked to this one supplier.

12 min read2026-07-07

What you are actually buying

Nvidia is no longer a chip company in any meaningful sense; it is the landlord of AI compute. In its fiscal 2025 — the year ending January 2025 — it reported $130.5 billion of revenue, up 114% year on year, of which $115.2 billion came from a single segment: the data center.1 A year later, fiscal 2026 revenue reached $215.9 billion, with $193.7 billion of it from data center.6 No company of this size has ever grown this fast, and almost none has ever concentrated so much of its fortune in one product line sold to a handful of customers.

$193.7BDATA-CENTER REVENUE, FY2026
~90%OF TOTAL COMPANY REVENUE
75.0%GAAP GROSS MARGIN, FY2025
~13%OF REVENUE FROM CHINA, FY2025

For a capital allocator, three questions decide whether this is a franchise or a bubble, and this dossier takes them in order. How good is the data-center business, really? What actually stops a competitor from taking it — the "moat," named precisely rather than admired vaguely? And what is the one risk the moat cannot cover: the US government's power to decide who Nvidia may sell to. A patient owner needs a defensible answer to all three.3

The data-center machine

Begin with the economics, because they are the whole story. In fiscal 2025 the data-center segment grew 142% to $115.2 billion; the next-largest segment, gaming, was $11.35 billion, with professional visualization at $1.88 billion and automotive at $1.69 billion.19 Data center is not the largest part of Nvidia; it is roughly nine-tenths of it, and everything else is a rounding error by comparison.

The growth has been close to vertical. Quarterly data-center revenue climbed from $18.4 billion at the start of calendar 2024 to $62.3 billion by the quarter ending January 2026 — more than a tripling in two years.67

That concentration cuts two ways an owner should weigh. The revenue leans on a small number of very large buyers — the hyperscale cloud providers — and Nvidia's own 10-K flags significant customer concentration and a heavy geographic tilt, with a large share of sales billed through a handful of countries.3 A business whose demand sits with perhaps five customers, several of whom are simultaneously building rival chips, is powerful and fragile in the same breath: any one of them trimming orders moves the whole company.

What makes the segment remarkable is not the growth but the margin at which it grows. Nvidia's GAAP gross margin rose to 75.0% in fiscal 2025, from 72.7% the year before, which the company attributed to a richer mix of data-center sales.3 A hardware maker earning 75 cents of gross profit on every revenue dollar, while doubling volume, is a company facing no effective price competition. The reason it faces none is the subject of the next section — but the caveat that comes with it is visible in the same filings. Management has been guiding margins back toward the low-70s as it ships more complex, lower-margin rack-scale systems (the Blackwell "NVL72" racks that bundle GPUs, CPUs, and networking into one unit), and, more sharply, as a one-time charge tied to China cut a single quarter's GAAP margin to 60.5%.24 Hold that thread; it leads directly to the export-control section.

The moat, named precisely

"Nvidia has a strong moat" is not analysis. The moat has a specific mechanism, and it is worth naming exactly, because the durability of the whole investment case rests on it. It is a two-sided ecosystem lock-in with a high switching cost, and its name is CUDA.

CUDA Nvidia's proprietary software platform, launched in 2006, that lets developers run general-purpose parallel code on Nvidia GPUs — and only on Nvidia GPUs. It is the layer that turns the silicon into something programmable, and it does not run on anyone else's chips.

The lock works in three reinforcing layers. First, the libraries. On top of CUDA sit the tools practitioners actually call — cuDNN for neural networks, cuBLAS for linear algebra, TensorRT for inference — part of what Nvidia says are more than 900 "CUDA-X" libraries.15 The dominant machine-learning frameworks are tuned first and best against these. Porting a large training or inference stack to a competitor's software means rewriting and re-validating against a less mature alternative, and the engineering teams capable of doing that are scarce and expensive. That is the switching cost, and it is measured in quarters of lost work, not dollars off a price list.

Second, the flywheel. Nvidia's Jensen Huang has said some six million developers across more than 200 countries have used CUDA since 2006, atop an installed base of hundreds of millions of compatible GPUs.15 More developers write more software for the platform, which makes the platform more valuable, which draws more developers — a compounding loop that a challenger cannot short-circuit by simply building a faster chip, because the chip is not what customers are locked to.

Third, the system. Nvidia has extended the moat from the chip to the whole rack. Its $6.9 billion acquisition of Mellanox, announced in 2019 and closed in April 2020, brought InfiniBand and Ethernet networking in-house;10 on top of that sit NVLink, the high-speed interconnect that lets dozens of GPUs act as one, and a growing software-and-networking stack sold as a complete "AI factory" rather than a component. The pitch to a buyer is no longer "buy our GPU" but "buy our rack, our network, and our software, all co-designed." The competitive effect is that Nvidia holds an estimated 80–90% of the AI-accelerator market by revenue, with training share above 90%.18 That is not a lead; it is, for now, a near-monopoly on the picks and shovels of the entire industry.

The one risk the moat cannot patch: export controls

A software moat defends against competitors. It does nothing against a government that can simply forbid a sale. The single largest discrete risk to Nvidia is not AMD or a custom chip; it is the US Department of Commerce's Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS), which regulates what advanced computing hardware Nvidia may export, and to whom, under the Export Administration Regulations. The rules bind Nvidia as a US exporter, they have tightened roughly once a year since 2022, and each turn has cost the company a redesign or a writedown.

Export Administration Regulations (EAR) The US rulebook, enforced by the Commerce Department's BIS, that controls exports of sensitive technology. For advanced AI chips it sets performance and interconnect thresholds; a chip above them cannot be shipped to specified countries — chiefly China — without a license.

The timeline is a game of the government raising the bar and Nvidia limbo-ing under it. On 7 October 2022, BIS issued its first advanced-computing controls, which banned the sale of Nvidia's top A100 and H100 accelerators to China; Nvidia responded with cut-down A800 and H800 parts engineered to fall just under the thresholds.1112 On 17 October 2023, BIS tightened the rules — a new "performance density" metric, the interconnect loophole closed — banning the A800 and H800 in turn.1314 Nvidia then built the further-degraded H20 specifically for the Chinese market.

In 2025 the vise closed on that chip too, and this is where the policy risk stops being abstract and starts showing up in the accounts. On 9 April 2025 the government told Nvidia that exporting the H20 to China would now require a license, and on 14 April it said the requirement would stand "for the indefinite future."5 Nvidia disclosed it would take a charge of up to roughly $5.5 billion.5 When the quarter closed, the actual charge was $4.5 billion, and the company said roughly $8.0 billion of H20 revenue had been stripped from the following quarter's guidance — the write-down that pushed GAAP gross margin down to 60.5% for the period.4

Then the politics turned again. Around mid-July 2025 the administration signalled it would grant H20 export licenses after all,21 and in August it emerged on unusual terms: Nvidia (and AMD) would remit 15% of their China H20 revenue to the US government as a condition of the licenses — a novel "pay-to-export" arrangement with no clear precedent.20 For an owner, the lesson is not which way the last ruling went but the structural fact underneath: a market that was about 13% of revenue in fiscal 2025 is now a binary, politically-controlled variable that Nvidia's guidance has at times assumed at zero.3 The moat holds everywhere except here.

The bull and the bear, at their strongest

A reader should not be able to tell which way this dossier privately leans, so here are both cases in the words of their most credible holders. The bull case rests on the sheer scale and duration of the buildout; the bear case rests on three specific cracks — custom silicon, margin normalization, and the possibility that the whole cycle is overbuilt.

Morgan Stanley (bull) The market still underestimates how large and how long the AI-infrastructure buildout runs.

The bank has carried a bull-case target around $330 and estimated Nvidia could generate on the order of $1.07 trillion in cumulative data-center revenue across calendar 2025–2027; Wedbush's Dan Ives has argued similarly for a $250 base case.19

Daniel Newman, Futurum Group (bear on share) Custom chips built by Nvidia's own biggest customers will grow faster than the merchant GPU market and erode its share.

Newman has argued cloud ASIC shipments are growing faster than GPUs; the named programs are real and shipping — Google's TPU, Amazon's Trainium, Microsoft's Maia — and each hyperscaler that moves inference to its own silicon is a customer Nvidia partly loses. AMD's MI300-class parts add a second front.1822

Michael Burry, Scion (bear on the cycle) This is a capex bubble with Nvidia as its Cisco, and the hardware's true economics are being flattered.

Disclosing a short position in 2025, Burry called Nvidia "a Cisco at the center" of the buildout and argued that hyperscalers depreciate GPUs over four-to-six years when the real useful life, given Nvidia's annual cadence, is nearer two-and-a-half to three-and-a-half — understating costs and setting up writedowns. Nvidia pushed back publicly on the "bubble" framing.1617

The honest synthesis is that both sides are describing the same fact from opposite ends. Nvidia's margins and share are extraordinary because the moat is real; those same extraordinary numbers are what draw the capital — custom silicon, AMD, and the hyperscalers' own chip teams — that will, in time, compete them down. Which force wins, and when, is the actual investment question. Nothing in the filings settles it.

Go deeper

Do not take this dossier's numbers on faith; the primary documents are public and readable. Start with Nvidia's most recent 10-K and quarterly results on SEC EDGAR and the investor site for the segment detail and the margin bridge.31 For the export-control mechanism, read the BIS press releases and the Congressional Research Service's timeline rather than the coverage of them, so you can see exactly which threshold moved and when.1113 To judge the moat for yourself, watch one metric each quarter that the headlines skip: gross margin. It is the single cleanest signal of whether the pricing power — and therefore the moat — is intact, softening under rack-scale mix, or breaking under competition. When that number moves durably, the thesis moves with it.2

Sources

  1. SEC EDGAR — Nvidia 8-K, "Financial Results for Q4 and Fiscal 2025". FY2025 total revenue $130.5B (+114%), data center $115.2B (+142%); Q4 data center $35.6B.
  2. SEC EDGAR — Nvidia CFO Commentary, Q4 / Fiscal 2025. Gross-margin drivers and the rack-scale (Blackwell NVL72) mix headwind.
  3. SEC EDGAR — Nvidia Form 10-K, fiscal year ended January 26, 2025. GAAP gross margin 75.0% (vs 72.7% FY2024); China revenue ~$17.1B (~13% of total); customer and geographic concentration.
  4. SEC EDGAR — Nvidia 8-K, "Financial Results for Q1 Fiscal 2026". Q1 FY2026 data center $39.1B; GAAP gross margin 60.5%; $4.5B H20 charge; ~$8.0B of H20 revenue removed from Q2 guidance; ~71.3% margin ex-charge.
  5. SEC EDGAR — Nvidia 8-K, April 15, 2025 (H20 charge disclosure). April 9 license requirement, April 14 "indefinite," and the "up to approximately $5.5 billion" charge.
  6. SEC EDGAR — Nvidia 8-K, "Financial Results for Q4 and Fiscal 2026". FY2026 total revenue $215.9B (+65%), data center $193.7B (+68%); Q4 data center $62.3B.
  7. SEC EDGAR — Nvidia 8-K, "Financial Results for Q3 Fiscal 2026". Q3 FY2026 data-center revenue $51.2B.
  8. SEC EDGAR — Nvidia 8-K, "Financial Results for Q2 Fiscal 2026". Q2 FY2026 data-center revenue $41.1B.
  9. Nvidia Newsroom — "NVIDIA Announces Financial Results for Fourth Quarter and Fiscal 2025". Segment breakdown: gaming $11.35B, professional visualization $1.88B, automotive $1.69B.
  10. Nvidia Newsroom — "NVIDIA to Acquire Mellanox for $6.9 Billion". The 2019 networking acquisition (closed April 2020) underpinning InfiniBand/NVLink.
  11. US BIS (Commerce) — "Commerce Strengthens Export Controls…" (October 7, 2022). The initial advanced-computing controls that banned A100/H100 to China.
  12. Federal Register — "Implementation of Additional Export Controls: Certain Advanced Computing and Semiconductor Manufacturing Items". The codified 2022 rule text (thresholds, covered countries).
  13. Congressional Research Service — R48642, "U.S. Export Controls and China: Advanced Semiconductors". The Oct 2022 → Oct 2023 → 2025 timeline; the A800/H800 loophole and its closure.
  14. CSET, Georgetown — "The Commerce Department's October 2023 Export Control Update". The performance-density metric and the A800/H800 ban.
  15. Transcript — Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, GTC 2025 keynote. ~6 million CUDA developers since 2006; 900+ CUDA-X libraries; installed-base flywheel framing.
  16. Fortune — "'Big Short' investor Michael Burry on an AI bubble: '…Its name is Nvidia'" (2025). The Cisco analogy, GPU-depreciation argument, and short position.
  17. CNBC — "Nvidia name-checks Michael Burry in memo pushing back on AI bubble allegations" (2025). Nvidia's rebuttal to the bubble thesis.
  18. CNBC — "Nvidia sales are 'off the charts,' but Google, Amazon and others now make their own custom AI chips" (2025). Daniel Newman on ASIC growth; Google TPU, AWS Trainium, Microsoft Maia; ~80–90% accelerator share.
  19. TheStreet — "Morgan Stanley sets bold new price target on Nvidia stock". Bull-case ~$330 and ~$1.07T cumulative data-center revenue CY2025–27; Wedbush's Dan Ives $250.
  20. NPR — "Trump says Nvidia will hand the U.S. 15% of its H20 chip sales to China" (August 11, 2025). The 15% revenue-share condition on China H20 licenses.
  21. Bloomberg — "Nvidia Expects License to Sell H20 AI Chip to China Again" (July 15, 2025). The mid-2025 signal that H20 licenses would resume.
  22. DataCenterDynamics — "AMD posts record data center revenue for Q3 2025". AMD's MI300-class ramp as the second competitive front.

entry.title: The Wires Between the Chips
entry.summary: AI's next bottleneck is not the chip but the cable between chips — and an industry coalition is trying to pry that cable open before Nvidia owns it.
entry.content:
The Wildcard Desk

The Wires Between the Chips

The fight that will shape AI economics next is not about faster chips but about who controls the fabric that wires thousands of them into one machine.

7 min read2026-07-07

The bottleneck nobody photographs

Every photograph of the AI boom shows a chip. The real constraint is increasingly the wire beside it. A frontier model is too large to fit on one accelerator, so training and inference split it across dozens or thousands of them, and at every step those chips must swap enormous volumes of data. When the wire between them cannot keep up, the chips sit idle waiting — and an idle GPU is the most expensive thing in a data center. Nvidia says it plainly: its 72-GPU rack behaves as "a single, massive GPU," and that is a claim about the fabric, not the silicon.2

Scale-up vs scale-out Scale-up is the tight, low-latency fabric that fuses a rack of accelerators into one unit — Nvidia's NVLink territory. Scale-out is the wider network that links those racks into a cluster of thousands, usually over Ethernet or InfiniBand. Different fabrics, different problems, and — until recently — different owners.

Nvidia owns the most valuable piece of this plumbing, the scale-up fabric, and owns it proprietarily. In mid-2024 a coalition of nearly every other major chip and cloud company set out to build an open replacement, and in 2025 the specifications actually arrived. This is an explainer for the reader who keeps hearing "NVLink" and "UALink" and wants to know what is genuinely at stake — and whether the open challenge is real.1

Why the wires decide the economics

Training a large model means constantly averaging results across all the chips working on it — an operation where every accelerator sends data to every other one, over and over. As the chips themselves get faster, the wire between them becomes the limiting factor: the compute finishes and then waits on the network. The whole game is keeping the fabric ahead of the silicon.1

1.8 TB/sNVLINK BANDWIDTH PER GPU (5TH GEN)
72GPUS IN ONE NVLINK DOMAIN (NVL72)
1,024ACCELERATORS PER UALINK POD (SPEC)

Nvidia's answer is NVLink, now in its fifth generation, delivering up to 1.8 terabytes per second of bandwidth to each GPU — far more than any general-purpose network offers.1 Paired with its NVSwitch chips, NVLink lets 72 Blackwell GPUs in a single rack — the "GB200 NVL72" — address one another at full speed, so software can treat the rack as one enormous processor.2 That is the technical marvel. It is also the moat: NVLink is proprietary and closed, so no third party's accelerator can join an NVLink domain. A customer who wants the "one giant GPU" experience must buy the entire Nvidia rack — chips, switches, and software together. The fabric is where the lock-in lives.

The open coalition

To pry that fabric open, Nvidia's largest customers and rivals have split the problem in two and built a standards body for each half. Both delivered version-1.0 specifications in 2025 — the point at which a coalition stops being a press release and becomes something silicon can be built against.

UALink ConsortiumThe open scale-up standard — the direct NVLink challenger
Ultra Ethernet ConsortiumThe open scale-out standard — Ethernet rebuilt for AI
NvidiaThe incumbent defending the proprietary fabric
BroadcomThe swing player — in both consortia, selling its own Ethernet

The scale-up challenger is the Ultra Accelerator Link (UALink) Consortium, which incorporated in October 2024 and published its "200G 1.0" specification on 8 April 2025. The spec defines a low-latency interconnect running 200 billion transfers per second per lane, and lets a single pod connect up to 1,024 accelerators — an open, multi-vendor answer to the closed NVLink domain.3 Its promoter roster is a who's-who of everyone-but-Nvidia: AMD, Apple, Astera Labs, AWS, Cisco, Google, HPE, Intel, Meta, Microsoft, Synopsys and Alibaba, atop more than eighty member companies.4

The scale-out challenger is the Ultra Ethernet Consortium (UEC), a Linux Foundation project announced in July 2023 by AMD, Arista, Broadcom, Cisco, Eviden, HPE, Intel, Meta and Microsoft.6 Its 1.0 specification, released on 11 June 2025 and running past 560 pages, rebuilds Ethernet's entire stack — network cards, switches, optics, and a new transport layer — for AI traffic, so that many racks can be wired into one cluster over open, multi-vendor Ethernet rather than Nvidia's InfiniBand.5 Together, UALink for scale-up and UEC for scale-out are the two halves of a single strategy: replace Nvidia's vertically integrated fabric with open standards at both layers.

Nvidia's answer, and Broadcom's twist

Nvidia did not sit still. At Computex in May 2025 it announced NVLink Fusion, which licenses the NVLink fabric to third parties so that custom accelerators and non-Nvidia CPUs can plug into an Nvidia rack — a controlled opening of the moat, on Nvidia's terms rather than the consortium's. Its first partners include MediaTek, Marvell, Alchip, Astera Labs, Synopsys and Cadence, with Fujitsu and Qualcomm CPUs able to join too.7 The move co-opts the very demand — hyperscalers wanting their own silicon in the rack — that UALink was built to serve.

On the scale-out side, Nvidia is not ceding Ethernet either: its Spectrum-X platform brings AI-tuned features to Ethernet directly, competing on the exact terrain the Ultra Ethernet Consortium aims to open.8 The result is that Nvidia has an answer at both layers of the coalition's attack.

The most revealing complication comes from inside the coalition. Broadcom is a member of both UALink and UEC, yet it is aggressively pushing its own Ethernet silicon into the scale-up tier the consortium reserved for UALink. Its Tomahawk 6 switch — the first to hit 102.4 terabits per second on a single chip — can wire 512 accelerators into a scale-up topology, and its "Tomahawk Ultra" is explicitly pitched as an Ethernet switch reimagined for scale-up.910 The coalition's most important merchant-silicon vendor, in other words, would rather sell its own Ethernet than wait for UALink — a fracture line running through the open camp itself.

Will it actually matter?

The strongest fact for the skeptics is timing. Nvidia's 72-GPU NVLink rack has been shipping since 2024;2 the UALink 1.0 specification only published in April 2025, and silicon that implements a spec typically trails it by 18 to 24 months.3 For at least one more product generation, in other words, the open scale-up fabric has no shipping product to point to, while Nvidia sells a mature one today and has already moved to license it selectively.

The bull case for the coalition is structural: the buyers are the sellers. The same hyperscalers designing their own accelerators — Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Meta — have every incentive to avoid being locked to a single supplier's fabric, and they sit on UALink's board. If they deploy open pods at scale, the standard succeeds by fiat of the purchasing budget. The question is whether they do that, or whether NVLink Fusion and their own custom-Ethernet options prove easier than waiting for a young multi-vendor standard to mature.

A falsifiable claim to grade later3

No commercially purchasable, multi-vendor UALink system connecting more than 100 accelerators in a single scale-up pod ships to customers before the end of 2026 — while Nvidia's 72-GPU NVLink domain has shipped since 2024. The spec-to-volume-silicon lag makes a shipping open pod this year unlikely; a purchasable UALink pod above that size before the deadline falsifies it.

confidence 70%resolves by 2026-12-31

Go deeper

The primary documents are unusually readable for a standards fight. Start with the UALink Consortium's own specification page and the Ultra Ethernet Consortium's 1.0 announcement to see exactly what each covers and who signed on.45 Then read Nvidia's NVLink Fusion announcement as the incumbent's counter-move, and watch one signal over the next year: whether any hyperscaler announces a production cluster built on UALink rather than NVLink.7 That single deployment — or its continued absence — will tell you more about who owns the AI fabric than any spec sheet. This desk will grade the claim above when the year closes.

Sources

  1. NVIDIA — "NVLink and NVLink Switch". Fifth-generation NVLink bandwidth of up to 1.8 TB/s per GPU; why interconnect bandwidth is the constraint.
  2. NVIDIA — "GB200 NVL72". The 72-GPU NVLink domain that "acts as a single, massive GPU"; shipping rack-scale product.
  3. Business Wire — "UALink Consortium Releases the Ultra Accelerator Link 200G 1.0 Specification" (April 8, 2025). The spec: 200 GT/s per lane, up to 1,024 accelerators per pod; incorporated October 2024.
  4. UALink Consortium — "Specifications". The open scale-up specification and its promoter and member roster (80+ companies).
  5. Ultra Ethernet Consortium — "UEC Launches Specification 1.0" (June 11, 2025). The full-stack Ethernet spec (NICs, switches, optics, transport) for AI/HPC scale-out.
  6. Linux Foundation — "Announcing the Ultra Ethernet Consortium" (July 2023). Formation and founding members (AMD, Arista, Broadcom, Cisco, Eviden, HPE, Intel, Meta, Microsoft).
  7. NVIDIA Newsroom — "NVIDIA Unveils NVLink Fusion" (Computex, May 2025). Licensing NVLink to third-party CPUs and accelerators; the named partner ecosystem.
  8. NVIDIA — "Spectrum-X Ethernet Platform". NVIDIA's own AI-optimized Ethernet, its scale-out hedge against open Ethernet.
  9. Broadcom — "Broadcom Ships Tomahawk 6: World's First 102.4 Tbps Switch". A single-chip switch that can wire 512 accelerators in a scale-up topology.
  10. Broadcom — "Broadcom Ships Tomahawk Ultra: Reimagining the Ethernet Switch for HPC and AI Scale-Up". Broadcom's "scale-up Ethernet," competing with UALink despite its membership.

entry.title: Attention Is All You Need (2017)
entry.summary: The eight pages that redrew the field — and which of their specific bets have actually held up nine years on.
entry.content:
Landmark Papers

Attention Is All You Need (2017)

The architecture bet aged extraordinarily well; several of the paper's specific engineering choices did not.

8 min read2026-07-07

What this paper was, and why it still matters

In June 2017, eight researchers at Google published a paper with a title that reads, in hindsight, like a manifesto: Attention Is All You Need.1 It proposed an architecture — the Transformer — that has since become the substrate of almost every frontier AI system in language, vision, and biology. This appraisal asks the only question worth asking about a nine-year-old landmark: not whether it mattered, but which of its specific claims have survived contact with everything published since.

In plain language

At the time, the best translation systems read a sentence word by word, in order, using networks that passed a running memory forward. This paper threw out that sequential machinery and replaced it with a single mechanism — attention — that lets every word look directly at every other word at once. The result beat the state of the art on two standard translation benchmarks while training in a fraction of the time, because the new design could be spread across many processors at once.1

A note on scope, because the paper's reputation now vastly exceeds what it actually claimed. This was a paper about machine translation. It said nothing about pretraining, general-purpose models, scaling laws, or "foundation models" — all of that came later. Read fairly, it is the seed, not the harvest.1

What it actually demonstrated

Strip away the reputation and the paper is a tight empirical result on two 2014 translation tasks. Its "big" model reached a BLEU score — the standard 0–100 measure of translation quality against human references — of 28.4 on English-to-German and 41.8 on English-to-French, both new states of the art; the smaller "base" model scored 27.3 and 38.1.1 The base model held roughly 65 million parameters, the big one 213 million.1

The headline was not only quality but cost. The big model reached that French result after 3.5 days on eight NVIDIA P100 GPUs — a small fraction of the compute rival systems needed.1 The peer-reviewed NeurIPS version carried the same numbers.2

Self-attention A layer in which every position in a sequence computes a weighted combination of all other positions, the weights set by learned similarity. No recurrence, no convolution — every token connects to every other in one step.

The mechanism the paper leans on for its argument is a comparison table that is easy to miss and central to everything. It scores layer types on three axes. A recurrent layer needs O(n) sequential operations for a sentence of n tokens — steps that must happen one after another — and up to n hops for one word to influence another. Self-attention needs O(1) sequential operations and connects any two positions in a single hop.1 That is the whole case: the same expressive power, but parallelizable, so you can train it faster and scale it further.

Crucially, the paper was honest about the catch. Self-attention costs O(n²·d) per layer — the work grows with the square of sequence length — and it noted this is cheaper than recurrence only when the sentence is shorter than the model is wide, "which is most often the case" for the sentence-length inputs it studied.1 That quiet caveat is where much of the paper's later trouble — and a whole research field — would grow.

How it has aged

The architectural bet paid off on a scale the authors did not claim. Within a year, BERT showed that pretraining a Transformer encoder on unlabeled text and fine-tuning it set new records across language understanding, pushing the GLUE benchmark to 80.5%.3 By 2020, GPT-3 was a 175-billion-parameter Transformer that learned tasks from a few examples in its prompt,4 and Kaplan and colleagues had shown that Transformer loss falls as a smooth power law across more than seven orders of magnitude of model size, data, and compute — turning "make it bigger" into a principled program.5 The architecture then jumped domains: a pure Transformer on image patches matched the best convolutional networks,6 and AlphaFold2's attention-based network predicted protein structures at accuracy competitive with experiment.7 Almost nothing in that list is machine translation, and all of it runs on the block this paper defined.

But a fair appraisal separates the durable core from the specific choices that were quietly replaced. Three of the paper's particulars did not survive.

First, the O(n²) cost it flagged became the field's central bottleneck as context windows grew, and it spawned a large "efficient attention" literature: Longformer made attention scale linearly with a sliding window,8 Performer approximated it in linear time,9 and FlashAttention made exact attention memory-linear by rethinking how it moves data on the GPU.10 That last point is the sharp one: much of the Transformer's later practicality came from systems engineering the 2017 paper never anticipated.

Second, its positional scheme was superseded. The paper's own ablation found learned and fixed sinusoidal position encodings "nearly identical,"1 and modern models discarded both in favor of rotary embeddings (RoPE), now standard in open LLMs,11 or ALiBi, which lets a model trained on short sequences run on longer ones.12 Third, the intuitive promise that attention weights show what a model "looks at" was actively contested: Jain and Wallace argued the weights are not faithful explanations,13 and Wiegreffe and Pinter rebutted that it depends on what you mean by explanation.14 The debate remains unsettled.

What holds up
  • The Transformer block — multi-head self-attention, a position-wise feed-forward network, residual connections — is essentially unchanged at the core of frontier models today.4
  • The parallelization argument was correct and load-bearing: removing recurrence is what let the architecture scale with compute.5
  • The bet generalized far beyond translation — to language understanding, vision, and structural biology.67
What to be careful about
  • The vanilla O(n²) attention it shipped is not what runs at scale today; efficient and IO-aware variants do.10
  • Its sinusoidal positions were replaced by RoPE and ALiBi.1112
  • Its ablations were modest — one language pair, small deltas — and it offered no scaling study; the factors that later mattered most were untested here.5

How much weight to put on it today

The honest reading holds two facts at once. The paper's architectural contribution is among the most important in the field's history and is still live in production. Its account of why Transformers win is thin, because the decisive ingredients — scale, pretraining objectives, modern positional schemes, GPU-aware kernels — are all post-2017. Treat it as the founding blueprint, not the finished explanation.

Verdict Put maximum weight on the Transformer block as a design and near-zero weight on the paper as a complete theory of modern AI. The architecture is the seed of the era; the harvest was grown by a decade of work the paper did not foresee.5

What would change this assessment? A non-attention architecture — a state-space model in the Mamba lineage, or a recurrent-retrieval hybrid — decisively beating Transformers at frontier scale across modalities would retroactively reframe this paper as an important-but-transitional step rather than a permanent foundation.6 Conversely, continued dominance of the unmodified attention core through the next model generation strengthens the "landmark that defined the era" reading. As of 2026, the core still stands.

Go deeper

Read the original first; it is eight pages and, with this appraisal in hand, the Table 1 complexity argument and the ablations in Table 3 are the parts to dwell on.1 Then trace the lineage forward through the two papers that turned the architecture into a program — GPT-3 for in-context learning4 and Kaplan et al. for the scaling laws that justified the buildout.5 To understand what actually changed under the hood, FlashAttention is the clearest case study in how systems work, not new math, unlocked long context.10 Finally, read the "attention is (not) explanation" exchange as a pair — it is a model of how a field litigates an intuition it once took for granted.1314

Sources

  1. arXiv — Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need" (2017). The paper under review: BLEU 28.4/41.8 (big) and 27.3/38.1 (base), 65M/213M parameters, 3.5 days on 8 P100 GPUs (§6, Table 2); the O(n²·d) cost and O(1)-vs-O(n) sequential-operations and path-length comparison (Table 1); the learned-vs-sinusoidal ablation (Table 3).
  2. NeurIPS 2017 Proceedings — "Attention Is All You Need" (published PDF). The peer-reviewed version of the same results.
  3. arXiv — Devlin et al., "BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers" (2018). Transformer-encoder pretraining; GLUE score raised to 80.5%.
  4. arXiv — Brown et al., "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners" (GPT-3, 2020). A 175-billion-parameter decoder Transformer with in-context learning; the transformer block essentially unchanged.
  5. arXiv — Kaplan et al., "Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models" (2020). Power-law scaling of loss in model size, data, and compute over >7 orders of magnitude.
  6. arXiv — Dosovitskiy et al., "An Image is Worth 16x16 Words" (ViT, 2020). A pure Transformer on image patches matching or beating convolutional networks at scale.
  7. Nature — Jumper et al., "Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold" (2021). An attention-based network achieving CASP14 accuracy competitive with experiment.
  8. arXiv — Beltagy et al., "Longformer: The Long-Document Transformer" (2020). Sliding-window plus global attention scaling linearly in sequence length.
  9. arXiv — Choromanski et al., "Rethinking Attention with Performers" (2020). Linear-time/space approximation of softmax attention.
  10. arXiv — Dao et al., "FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness" (2022). IO-aware exact attention with memory linear in sequence length.
  11. arXiv — Su et al., "RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding" (2021). RoPE, which superseded sinusoidal position encodings in modern LLMs.
  12. arXiv — Press et al., "Train Short, Test Long" (ALiBi, 2021). Distance-biased attention enabling length extrapolation.
  13. arXiv — Jain & Wallace, "Attention is not Explanation" (2019). Evidence that attention weights are not faithful explanations.
  14. arXiv — Wiegreffe & Pinter, "Attention is not not Explanation" (2019). The rebuttal: whether weights explain depends on the definition of explanation.

entry.title: Attention Is the Engine
entry.summary: Lesson 1: how a single arithmetic trick lets a model read every word at once, and why it is the reason modern AI works.
entry.content:
Transformers, From Scratch · Lesson 1

Attention Is the Engine

One arithmetic trick lets a model read every word of a sentence at the same time — and it is the reason modern AI works.

10 min read2026-07-07

Where we are starting

This course builds a modern language model from the ground up, one idea at a time, for a reader who has never trained a neural network. We start where the field itself restarted: a 2017 paper from Google researchers titled, with unusual confidence, Attention Is All You Need.1 Almost every system you have heard of — the model behind ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini — is a direct descendant of the eight-page architecture that paper described. If you understand its one central mechanism, called attention, the rest of the course is detail hung on that spine.

In this lesson
  • Explain, in plain words, why reading text one word at a time was the bottleneck attention removed.
  • Trace what the model actually computes when it "pays attention," using a single running sentence.
  • Read the real scaled-dot-product attention formula and say what each piece does.
  • Name the concrete numbers — parameters, context length — that connect the 2017 model to today's.

One term of art before we begin. A token is the unit a language model reads: roughly a word or a word-piece. "Attention is the engine" is five tokens. Everything that follows is about how a model decides which tokens matter to which.1

The problem: reading one word at a time

Before 2017, the best language models were recurrent networks. A recurrent network reads a sentence the way you might read a ticker tape: one token, then the next, carrying a running summary — a "hidden state" — forward as it goes. To use the tenth word it must first pass through the previous nine, in order.

That design has two costs, and the 2017 paper laid them out precisely in a comparison table.1 First, the work cannot be spread across many processors, because step ten depends on step nine: the paper counts a recurrent layer as needing O(n) sequential operations for a sentence of n tokens, meaning the number of unavoidable one-after-another steps grows with the length of the sentence. Second, for word ten to be influenced by word one, information has to survive nine hand-offs — a long chain along which meaning gets diluted.

The sequential bottleneck A recurrent model must process tokens in order, so long-range links between words are both slow to compute and easy to lose. Attention was designed to break exactly this constraint.

The fix the paper proposed sounds almost too simple: stop reading in order. Let every token look at every other token directly, in one parallel step. In the paper's own accounting, self-attention needs only O(1) sequential operations and connects any two positions with a path of length O(1) — a single hop instead of n.1 The question is what "looking at every other token" actually means as a calculation.

What "attention" actually is

Start with a sentence the original Google team used to explain their own model:6

"The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired."

What does "it" refer to? The animal. Change one word — "because it was too wide" — and "it" now means the street. To resolve the pronoun, the model has to let the word "it" inspect the other words in the sentence and decide which one it depends on. Google's own visualization of the model showed exactly this: when encoding "it," the network puts most of its weight on "animal" in the first sentence and shifts that weight to "street" in the second.6 That act — one token weighing all the others and pulling in information from the ones that matter — is attention.

To do this arithmetically, the model gives every token three vectors, each just a list of numbers it learns during training:

  • A query: what this token is looking for. ("I am a pronoun; who is my referent?")
  • A key: what this token offers to others. ("I am a noun, an animal.")
  • A value: the actual content this token contributes if attended to.
Self-attention Every token produces a query, a key, and a value. Each token's query is compared against every token's key to decide how much to borrow from every token's value. "Self" means the sentence is attending to itself.

The comparison is a dot product — multiply the query and key element by element and add up the result, a single number that is large when two vectors point the same way. "It" 's query lined up against "animal" 's key gives a high score; against "street" 's key, a low one. Those scores, once normalized, are the attention weights, and the model uses them to take a weighted average of the value vectors. High-scoring tokens dominate the average; the rest fade. That average is the new, context-aware representation of "it."1

Inside the mechanism, step by step

The 2017 paper compresses the whole operation into one line, which it calls scaled dot-product attention:1

Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QKᵀ / √dk) V

It looks dense, but you already understand every piece. Here it is as an ordered mechanism.

  1. Score every pair

    Multiply each query against every key (that is what QKᵀ does in one matrix step). For an n-token sentence you get an n×n grid of scores: how much each token should attend to each other token.1

  2. Divide by √dk

    Here dk is the length of the key vectors. The paper divides the scores by its square root because, for long vectors, raw dot products grow large and push the next step into a range where learning stalls; scaling keeps the numbers well-behaved. In the base model dk = 64, so the scores are divided by 8.1

  3. Softmax into weights

    The softmax function turns each row of scores into positive fractions that sum to one — a set of attention weights. Now "it" might place 0.7 of its attention on "animal" and spread the remaining 0.3 across the sentence.1

  4. Mix the values

    Multiply those weights by the value vectors and add them up. Each token walks away holding a blend of the tokens it cared about — context folded directly into its representation.1

One refinement makes it work in practice. A single attention pass can only track one kind of relationship at a time, but language has many at once — grammar, reference, tense. So the model runs several attention operations in parallel, each with its own learned queries, keys, and values, and stitches the results together. The paper calls this multi-head attention and uses h = 8 heads in its base model, each working in a 64-dimensional slice of the model's 512-dimensional representation.1 One head can specialize in "which noun does this pronoun point to" while another tracks "which verb governs this subject."

Multi-head attention Several attention operations run side by side, each learning a different type of relationship, then their outputs are combined. Eight heads in the original; up to 96 in GPT-3.3

That is the entire engine. The original Transformer stacked this block six times over in its encoder and six in its decoder, wrapped each with a small feed-forward network and some bookkeeping, and trained the result. At roughly 65 million adjustable numbers (parameters) for the base model and 213 million for the larger one, it reached a new state of the art on English-to-German and English-to-French translation — 28.4 and 41.8 on the standard BLEU quality scale — after just 3.5 days of training on eight GPUs.1 The point was not only that it was good, but that it was good cheaply, because attention parallelizes where recurrence cannot.

Why this one idea scaled the world

Because attention removes the sequential chain, you can pour far more computation at a model and it will actually use it. That is the thread connecting the 2017 model to everything since. The architecture barely changed; it just got bigger.

65MPARAMS · TRANSFORMER 2017
1.5BPARAMS · GPT-2 2019
175BPARAMS · GPT-3 2020

GPT-2, in 2019, was the same transformer block scaled to 1.5 billion parameters across 48 layers, reading up to 1,024 tokens at a time.45 A year later GPT-3 pushed to 175 billion parameters, 96 layers, and 96 attention heads, and consumed roughly 3.14×10²³ arithmetic operations — about 3,640 "petaflop/s-days" — to train.3 Nothing conceptual separates it from the 2017 model in this lesson; it is the same four steps, run wider and deeper.

There is one price for attention's power, and it sets up the rest of the course. Scoring every token against every other token means the work grows with the square of the sentence length — the paper lists self-attention's cost as O(n²·d) per layer.1 Double the context and you quadruple the attention cost. That quadratic wall is exactly why the length of text a model can hold in view — its context window — was a hard-won engineering prize, climbing from 2,048 tokens in GPT-3 to 128,000 in GPT-4 Turbo, 200,000 in Claude 3, and a million or more in Gemini 1.5.378910

  1. In the sentence "The trophy didn't fit in the suitcase because it was too small," which word does "it" refer to — and which of the three vectors (query, key, value) does "it" use to go looking?
  2. A recurrent network needs O(n) sequential steps for an n-token sentence; self-attention needs O(1). In one sentence, why does that difference matter for training speed?
  3. If a model's context window grows from 2,000 to 8,000 tokens, roughly how much more attention computation does each layer do — and why?
Next lesson We have the engine; next we build the car. "From GPT to Frontier Models" shows how stacking these attention blocks into a decoder and training it to predict the next token turns a translation architecture into a general-purpose model — and why "just predict the next word" turned out to be enough.

Go deeper

Read the eight pages that started it, then watch someone build it. The original paper is short and, with this lesson behind you, readable in an afternoon.1 For a visual walk through the query-key-value arithmetic, Jay Alammar's Illustrated Transformer is the standard first stop.11 When you want to see attention as running code, Andrej Karpathy builds a working GPT from an empty file in two hours,12 and Harvard NLP's Annotated Transformer pairs the paper line-by-line with its implementation.13 For pure intuition with no code, 3Blue1Brown's chapter on attention is the clearest picture of the mechanism drawn anywhere.14 Do one of these before the next lesson and the rest of the course will feel like recognition rather than introduction.

Sources

  1. arXiv — Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need" (2017). The attention formula and scaling (§3.2.1), multi-head attention with h=8 and d_k=64 (§3.2.2), the O(1)-vs-O(n) sequential-operations and path-length comparison and O(n²·d) cost (Table 1), 65M/213M parameters, BLEU 28.4/41.8 and 3.5 days on 8 GPUs (§6, Table 2).
  2. NeurIPS 2017 Proceedings — "Attention Is All You Need" (published PDF). The peer-reviewed version of the same architecture and results.
  3. arXiv — Brown et al., "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners" (GPT-3, 2020). 175B parameters, 96 layers, 96 heads, d_model 12,288, 2,048-token context (Table 2.1); ~3,640 petaflop/s-days of training compute (Appendix D).
  4. OpenAI — Radford et al., "Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners" (GPT-2). 1.5B parameters, 48 layers, 1,024-token context.
  5. OpenAI — "Better language models and their implications" (GPT-2 announcement, 2019). GPT-2's scale and training corpus.
  6. Google Research — "Transformer: A Novel Neural Network Architecture for Language Understanding" (2017). The "animal / street / it" pronoun example and the attention visualization.
  7. OpenAI — "New models and developer products announced at DevDay" (2023). GPT-4 Turbo's 128,000-token context window.
  8. Meta AI — "Introducing Llama 3.1" (2024). Llama 3.1's 128,000-token context window.
  9. Anthropic — "Introducing Claude 2.1" (2023). The 200,000-token context window carried into the Claude 3 family.
  10. Google — "Introducing Gemini 1.5" (2024). The 1,000,000-token context window.
  11. Jay Alammar — "The Illustrated Transformer". A visual, step-by-step walkthrough of query/key/value attention.
  12. Andrej Karpathy — "Let's build GPT: from scratch, in code, spelled out". Building a working GPT from an empty file.
  13. Harvard NLP — "The Annotated Transformer". The paper annotated line-by-line with a PyTorch implementation.
  14. 3Blue1Brown — "Attention in transformers, step-by-step". A pure-visual explanation of the attention mechanism.

entry.title: Daily Brief — 7 July 2026
entry.summary: Governance goes multilateral in Geneva, the best open-weights model is Chinese, and Tesla takes the driver out of the seat.
entry.content:
Daily Brief

Daily Brief — 7 July 2026

Six moves from the last few days: AI governance goes multilateral, open weights go Chinese, and the power grid becomes the real bottleneck.

4 min read2026-07-07
POLICY

The UN opens its first Global Dialogue on AI Governance in Geneva1

Running 6–7 July alongside the WSIS Forum and the AI for Good summit, the two-day session is the first meeting of the standing body created by last year's UN resolution, convening all 193 member states plus industry, academia and civil society. The Secretary-General used it to warn against letting AI "vibe-code" humanity's future.2

Why it matters → It establishes the first universal, all-member-state venue for AI governance — the multilateral counterweight to the EU's binding rulebook and Washington's lighter touch, and the reference point for coordination fights into 2027.

RESEARCH

China's Z.ai ships GLM-5.2, now the top open-weights model on several benchmarks3

Released under a permissive MIT license with a one-million-token context window, GLM-5.2 crested Western attention this week after independent benchmarks ranked it the strongest openly available model — beating GPT-5.5 on long-horizon coding tests at a fraction of the price.4

Why it matters → It reignites the "China is closing the gap cheaply" narrative that DeepSeek started, and the open weights plus low cost pressure the pricing and moats of the US frontier labs directly.

INDUSTRY

Anthropic launches Claude Science, a workbench aimed at researchers5

The beta bundles Claude with 60-plus curated skills and connectors for genomics, proteomics, structural biology and cheminformatics, renders scientific artifacts like 3D protein structures, and logs an auditable history of every result for reproducibility.

Why it matters → The bet is on workflow, not a new model — a sign that labs increasingly compete on integration into real scientific pipelines rather than on benchmark scores alone.

COMPUTE

National Grid puts $1.75 billion into a developer building dedicated AI power6

Announced 1 July, National Grid Ventures is taking a 35% stake in US developer Joulent, whose flagship Project Kilby is a 2.67-gigawatt West Texas generation campus — built 50/50 with Chevron — to supply a Microsoft data-center campus under a 20-year power deal from 2028.

Why it matters → It is concrete evidence that power, not chips, is now the binding constraint on AI buildout, and that operators are financing behind-the-meter generation to skip years-long grid-interconnection queues.

POLICY

The White House frontier-AI order moves toward "early access" for the government7

Under the 2 June executive order, federal agencies must stand up a voluntary framework by 1 August under which developers can give the government up to 30 days of pre-release access to "covered frontier models," tied to a classified, NSA-run process for benchmarking a model's cyber capabilities.

Why it matters → It would formalize in US policy the pre-release review that Anthropic and OpenAI already do informally — America's voluntary answer to the EU's binding obligations on general-purpose models.

WILDCARD

Tesla starts driverless Robotaxi rides in Miami — with no safety monitor8

On 3 July, Tesla began fully unsupervised rides in a roughly 10-to-14-square-mile geofence in western Miami-Dade, its first market outside Texas and California and the first where it skipped the human-monitored phase entirely — a detail confirmed by Tesla's VP of AI software on X rather than a formal release.

Why it matters → Removing the human monitor from day one, in a heavy-rain city, is a real-world stress test of camera-only autonomy and a bellwether for whether driverless services can scale beyond their careful first markets.

Sources

  1. United Nations — "Global Dialogue on AI Governance" (official event site, 6–7 July 2026). The first session, its participants, and its mandate.
  2. UN News — "Global push for AI governance amid warnings of 'catastrophic harm'". The Secretary-General's remarks at the opening.
  3. Z.ai / Hugging Face — "GLM-5.2: Built for Long-Horizon Tasks" (official release blog). MIT license, 1M-token context, and the model's benchmark claims.
  4. VentureBeat — "Z.ai's open-weights GLM-5.2 beats GPT-5.5 on multiple long-horizon coding benchmarks for 1/6th the cost". Independent benchmark ranking and cost comparison.
  5. Anthropic — "Claude Science, an AI workbench for scientists". The beta's features, skills, and reproducibility design.
  6. PR Newswire — "National Grid Ventures to invest $1.75bn to accelerate power solutions for U.S. data centers and AI". The Joulent stake and Project Kilby details.
  7. The White House — "Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security" (Executive Order, 2 June 2026). The voluntary early-access framework and the 1 August deadline.
  8. Not a Tesla App — "Tesla Launches Unsupervised Robotaxi Rides in Miami". The 3 July launch, the geofence, and the absence of a safety monitor.
